description: Growing data for all crops with timing relative to frost dates

tomato-beefsteak.name: Beefsteak Tomato
tips: Stake or cage plants when transplanting to avoid root damage later
Remove suckers for larger fruits, or leave for more but smaller tomatoes
Water deeply and consistently to prevent blossom end rot
Mulch heavily to retain moisture and prevent soil splash

Plant DEEP at transplant — strip the lower leaves until only the topmost 4–5 sets of leaves are left, then bury the rest of the stem underground. Roots form along the buried stem and make the plant much more drought-resistant.

pruning.title: Sucker your tomatoes
pruning.message: Indeterminate tomatoes (which beefsteaks are) need weekly suckering. A SUCKER is the shoot that grows UP from the 'V' where a side branch meets the main stem — pinch THAT off. The branch itself is the leafy growth going OUT to the side — don't pinch those, they make flowers and fruit.
pruning.actionHint: Look at the V where each branch meets the main stem. The upward-pointing shoot growing in that V crotch is the sucker — snap it off. Keep 1-2 main stems total. Pinch suckers when they're under 4 inches; bigger ones can be rooted to make new plants.

tomato-cherry.name: Cherry Tomatoes
tips: Very prolific - expect hundreds of fruits per plant
Can be grown in large containers on patios
Harvest when fully colored for best flavor
Plants continue producing until frost

Plant DEEP at transplant — strip the lower leaves until only the topmost 4–5 sets of leaves are left, then bury the rest of the stem underground. Roots form along the buried stem and make the plant much more drought-resistant.

pruning.title: Sucker your tomatoes
pruning.message: Find the V where each branch meets the main stem. The upward-pointing shoot growing in that V is the SUCKER — pinch it off. The branch itself (leafy growth going out to the side) is what makes fruit — leave those alone. Indeterminate cherry tomatoes branch a lot, so check weekly.
pruning.actionHint: Snap off suckers when they're under 4 inches (clean break, no torn tissue). Larger suckers can be rooted into new plants — stick a 6-inch sucker in moist soil and it'll root in a couple weeks.

tomato-heirloom.name: Heirloom Tomato
tips: More susceptible to disease than hybrids - good airflow is essential
Flavor is exceptional when vine-ripened
Handle fruits gently as skin is often thinner
Save seeds to continue the variety

Plant DEEP at transplant — strip the lower leaves until only the topmost 4–5 sets of leaves are left, then bury the rest of the stem underground. Roots form along the buried stem and make the plant much more drought-resistant.

pruning.title: Sucker pruning time
pruning.message: Heirlooms are usually indeterminate — they need suckering. A SUCKER is the upward-pointing shoot in the V where a side branch meets the main stem. Pinch those. The side branches themselves (going horizontally out) make flowers and fruit — leave them. Heirlooms have thinner skin and are more disease-prone, so airflow from suckering matters more here than for hybrids.
pruning.actionHint: Find each V crotch on the stem. Pinch off the upward-pointing shoots growing in those V's. Focus on suckers BELOW the first flower cluster. Repeat every 1-2 weeks. Use clean fingers/snips — torn tissue invites disease in humid weather.

tomato-roma.name: Roma Tomato
tips: Determinate variety - fruits ripen within a concentrated period
Excellent for canning and sauce making
Less juicy, more meaty flesh than slicing tomatoes
Good disease resistance

Plant DEEP at transplant — strip the lower leaves until only the topmost 4–5 sets of leaves are left, then bury the rest of the stem underground. Roots form along the buried stem and make the plant much more drought-resistant.

pruning.title: Light suckering for Romas
pruning.message: Check your Roma variety first — DETERMINATE Romas (Roma VF and many modern hybrids) should NOT be suckered at all because every shoot is a fruit cluster, and removing them removes your harvest. SEMI-DETERMINATE varieties tolerate light suckering of just the bottom 6-12 inches for airflow. If you don't know which you have, less is more.
pruning.actionHint: If determinate: do nothing. If semi-determinate or unknown: remove only the very bottom suckers (the ones in the V's below the first flower cluster) for airflow. Light pruning only — don't over-thin or you'll lose fruit.

pepper-bell-red.name: Red Bell Pepper
tips: Red peppers are fully ripe green peppers - takes 2-3 weeks longer
Sweeter and more nutritious than green peppers
Support plants once fruits develop to prevent breakage
Pick first fruits to encourage more production

pruning.title: Optional: pepper topping
pruning.message: Pepper topping is OPTIONAL — extension experts are split. Some report 15-20% more total fruit; others find no yield benefit and a 2-3 week delay in first harvest. Only top a plant that has 6-8 true leaves and NO flower buds yet. If yours is already flowering, skip topping this year.
pruning.actionHint: Verify: 6-8 true leaves, no flower buds visible. If both true: pinch the central growing tip. The plant branches into 2-4 stems within 10-14 days. If buds are already showing, leave it — topping a flowering pepper removes your first fruit set.

pepper-bell-green.name: Green Bell Pepper
tips: Harvest when full-sized and glossy for crisp texture
Can be picked at any size - smaller peppers are still tasty
Consistent watering prevents bitter taste
Plants are smaller than tomatoes but equally productive

pruning.title: Optional: pepper topping
pruning.message: Pepper topping is OPTIONAL — extension experts are split (some report ~15-20% more fruit; others find no benefit + a 2-3 week delay to first harvest). Only top a plant that has 6-8 true leaves and NO flower buds yet. If yours is already flowering, skip this year.
pruning.actionHint: Verify: 6-8 true leaves, no flower buds visible. If both true: pinch the central growing tip. Plant branches into 2-4 stems within 10-14 days. If buds are showing, leave it alone — topping a flowering pepper removes the first fruit set.

pepper-jalapeno.name: Jalapeno Pepper
tips: Stress (less water) increases heat level
White lines (corking) indicate a hotter pepper
Very prolific - expect 25-50 peppers per plant
Can be harvested green or red (red is sweeter and hotter)

pruning.title: Optional: pepper topping
pruning.message: Topping a jalapeno is OPTIONAL — extension sources are split on whether it actually increases yield. If you want to try it, only top a plant that has 6-8 true leaves and NO flower buds yet. If yours is already flowering, skip this year.
pruning.actionHint: Verify: 6-8 true leaves, no buds visible. If both true: pinch the central growing tip. Plant branches into 2-4 stems within 10-14 days. If buds are already there, leave it alone.

pepper-habanero.name: Habanero Pepper
tips: Needs long warm season - start early indoors
Extremely hot - use gloves when handling
Fruity flavor beneath the heat
Plants are beautiful with colorful fruits

pruning.title: Optional: habanero topping
pruning.message: Topping a habanero is OPTIONAL — they're slow growers, so the 2-3 week delay to first harvest matters more here than for faster peppers. Extension sources are split on whether topping actually increases yield. Only top a plant with 6-8 true leaves and NO flower buds.
pruning.actionHint: Verify: 6-8 true leaves, no buds visible. If both true: pinch the central growing tip. Plant branches into 2-4 stems within 10-14 days. If buds are already showing, skip this year — topping a flowering habanero will cost you the early harvest you need for a slow-grower.

pepper-serrano.name: Serrano Pepper
tips: Hotter than jalapenos, great for salsa
Very productive plants
Best harvested when still green and firm
Thinner walls than jalapenos - not ideal for stuffing

pruning.title: Optional: serrano topping
pruning.message: Topping a serrano is OPTIONAL — extension sources are split on yield benefit. Only top a plant that has 6-8 true leaves and NO flower buds yet. If yours is already flowering, skip topping this year.
pruning.actionHint: Verify: 6-8 true leaves, no buds visible. If both true: pinch the central growing tip. Plant branches into 2-4 stems within 10-14 days. If buds are already showing, leave it alone — topping a flowering plant removes your first fruit set.

eggplant.name: Eggplant
tips: Loves heat - don't rush transplanting
Harvest when skin is glossy; dull skin means overripe
Cut rather than pull fruits to avoid damaging plant
Flea beetles are common - use row cover early

pruning.title: Eggplant cleanup pruning
pruning.message: Extension publications (UMD, Cornell) do NOT recommend topping eggplant — it branches naturally with adequate spacing. The more impactful prune is removing yellowing lower leaves for airflow and limiting fruit count per plant to 4-6 so they size up.
pruning.actionHint: Remove yellowing or damaged lower leaves for airflow. Pinch any side shoots that emerge BELOW the first stem fork — those steal energy. Don't top the main stem; let it grow.

zucchini.name: Zucchini
tips: Incredibly prolific - 2-3 plants feed a family
Harvest at 6-8 inches for best texture
Check daily - fruits grow quickly
Male flowers appear first, then female flowers with tiny fruit

pruning.title: Old-leaf cleanup time
pruning.message: Remove yellowing or powdery-mildew-affected lower leaves to improve airflow and direct energy to fruit.
pruning.actionHint: Cut off lower leaves that are yellowing or showing mildew.

yellow-squash.name: Yellow Squash
tips: Same care as zucchini
Crookneck and straightneck varieties available
Harvest young for tender skin
Flowers are edible and delicious stuffed

pruning.title: Old-leaf cleanup time
pruning.message: Remove yellowing or affected lower leaves to keep airflow and prevent fungus spread.
pruning.actionHint: Cut off lower leaves that are yellowing or showing mildew.

cucumber.name: Cucumber
tips: Consistent watering prevents bitter taste
Trellis vining types to save space and keep fruits clean
Harvest frequently — every 1-2 days during peak. Fruit can go from ideal to over-ripe in 24 hours of hot weather.
Morning harvest gives crispest cucumbers

Yellowing = past prime. Once you see any yellow on a cucumber, harvest it IMMEDIATELY. Yellow means the plant has started developing seeds inside — flesh turns spongy and bitter, and the plant slows new fruit production to invest in seed maturation.

pruning.title: Pinch cucumber side shoots (vining varieties only)
pruning.message: This applies ONLY to VINING cucumbers (Marketmore, Diva, English types, anything that climbs a trellis). If yours is a BUSH variety (Bush Champion, Spacemaster, Bush Pickle), do NOT prune side shoots — that's where most of your fruit comes from. If you bought transplants and the label said 'bush' or 'compact,' skip this.
pruning.actionHint: VINING variety on a trellis: pinch any side shoots below the first 4–5 leaves to direct energy up the main vine. BUSH variety: do nothing — leave all the side shoots.

cucumber-pickling.name: Pickling Cucumber
tips: Harvest at 3-5 inches for whole pickles/gherkins; 5-7 inches for spear-cut pickles. Check every 1-2 days during peak.
Plants produce in waves - plan succession picklings
Bumpy skin is normal and desirable
Process within 24 hours of harvest for crispest pickles

Yellowing = past prime. The moment a pickling cucumber starts turning yellow, harvest it IMMEDIATELY — flesh gets bitter and spongy fast, and the plant slows new fruit production to invest in seed maturation. Pickle the yellowed ones for sauce/relish if anything, but ideally pick BEFORE they yellow.

pruning.title: Vine training time
pruning.message: Pickling cucumbers benefit from being trained up a trellis. Remove suckers below the first flower for better airflow.
pruning.actionHint: Tie main vine to trellis. Pinch suckers below first female flower.

butternut-squash.name: Butternut Squash
tips: Needs long warm season - start early in short-season areas
Cure in sun for 10 days after harvest for longer storage
Stores 3-6 months in cool, dry conditions
Leave 2-inch stem when harvesting

pruning.title: Vine training time
pruning.message: Train winter squash vines to keep them off other beds. Once 3-4 fruits are set (typically day 60-70), pinch the growing tips to focus the plant's energy on sizing those fruits up. Tip-pinching BEFORE fruit sets just shocks the plant.
pruning.actionHint: First: direct vines along the trellis or path you want. Then ONLY after 3-4 fruits are visibly sizing up (golf-ball or larger): pinch the very tip of each main vine.

acorn-squash.name: Acorn Squash
tips: Harvest when skin is hard and deep green with orange spot
Shorter storage life than butternut - use within 2 months
Single-serving size makes portioning easy
Can be baked whole, then scooped

spaghetti-squash.name: Spaghetti Squash
tips: Harvest when skin turns from green to yellow
Stores 1-2 months in cool conditions
Low-carb pasta alternative
Flesh forms spaghetti-like strands when cooked

pumpkin.name: Pumpkin
tips: Count back from desired harvest date to determine planting
Vines spread 15-20 feet - plan accordingly
Place cardboard under fruits to prevent rot
Cure 10 days in sun before storage

pruning.title: Vine training time
pruning.message: Pumpkins sprawl wide and set fruit around day 60-70. Direct vines while they're growing; ONLY after 2-3 pumpkins are visibly sizing up should you pinch the growing tips. Tip-pinching before fruit set just shocks the plant.
pruning.actionHint: First: lay vines where you want them (they self-root at leaf nodes, helping anchor). Then ONLY after 2-3 pumpkins are at least tennis-ball size: pinch the vine tip beyond the last good fruit.

watermelon.name: Watermelon
tips: Needs long hot season - use black plastic mulch to warm soil

Multiple ripeness signals (use 2+ together, not just one): (1) GROUND SPOT — the patch where the melon touches the ground turns from white to creamy yellow when ripe. This is the most reliable indicator. (2) TENDRIL — the curly tendril closest to the fruit dries up and turns brown. (3) THUMP TEST — ripe melon sounds dull/hollow, unripe sounds metallic/tight (use as confirmation, not primary).
Reduce watering during the last 2 weeks of ripening for sweeter flavor

pruning.title: Vine training time
pruning.message: Watermelon sets fruit around day 60-70. After 2-3 melons are visibly sizing up, pinch growing tips to focus the plant's energy. Tip-pinching BEFORE fruit sets just shocks the plant and delays everything.
pruning.actionHint: Wait until 2-3 melons are at least softball-sized. Then pinch the very tip of each main vine beyond the last fruit. This redirects sugar to the existing melons instead of starting new ones the season won't have time to ripen.

cantaloupe.name: Cantaloupe
tips: Ripe when stem slips easily from fruit with light pressure
Strong sweet aroma indicates ripeness
Net pattern becomes more pronounced when ripe
Don't refrigerate until cut for best flavor

pruning.title: Vine training time
pruning.message: Cantaloupe sets fruit around day 50-60. Direct vines onto a trellis while they grow. After 4 melons are visibly sizing up, pinch the growing tips so the plant focuses on the ones it has, not on starting new ones the season won't ripen.
pruning.actionHint: First: direct vines onto trellis or path. Then ONLY after 4 melons are at least softball-sized: pinch the very tip of each main vine beyond the last fruit.

honeydew.name: Honeydew Melon
tips: Needs longer season than cantaloupe
Ripe when blossom end gives slightly to pressure AND skin turns creamy yellow
Unlike cantaloupe, honeydew does NOT slip from the vine when ripe — you have to CUT it. Don't wait for it to drop or twist off; you'll miss the window.
Sweetest when left on vine until fully ripe (because it doesn't slip, it can stay on the vine longer than cantaloupe)

lettuce-romaine.name: Romaine Lettuce

fallSow.notes: Fall planting for late fall/winter harvest

tips: Bolt-resistant varieties extend harvest in warm weather
Harvest outer leaves for extended picking
Afternoon shade helps in hot weather
Succession plant every 2 weeks for continuous harvest

bolting.advice: Visible sign of bolting: a central flower stalk shoots STRAIGHT UP from the middle of the plant. Leaves may also start looking more pointed/elongated, and the plant gets taller and narrower. Once you see the stalk elongating, harvest the entire plant immediately — inner leaves may still be salvageable for a day or two, but outer leaves at the bottom are often already bitter. Sample a leaf: if it's bitter, time's up.

lettuce-butter.name: Butter Lettuce
tips: Tender leaves bruise easily - handle gently
Buttery, mild flavor
Harvest whole head or cut-and-come-again
Best in cooler weather

bolting.advice: Visible sign of bolting: a central stalk shoots straight up from the middle of the rosette. Leaves get pointier and the head loses its compact butterhead shape. Once you see the stalk elongating, harvest immediately — bitterness sets in within days. Butter lettuce bolts faster than most in heat (75°F+ sustained), so plant in afternoon shade for summer succession.

lettuce-mixed.name: Mixed Salad Greens
tips: Cut at 4-6 inches, will regrow 2-3 times
Mix includes variety of colors and textures
Great for containers and small spaces
Sow densely for baby greens

bolting.advice: Visible sign of bolting: any single leaf type in the mix shoots a central stalk straight up — pull that one and let the others keep going. Once half the bed is bolting, harvest the whole patch and reseed. Mixed greens bolt quickly; succession-plant every 2 weeks for steady harvest.

spinach.name: Spinach

fallSow.notes: Fall planting for winter harvest or overwintering

tips: Prefers cool weather - bolts quickly in heat
Plant in fall for overwintered spring harvest
High in iron and nutrients
Baby leaves are more tender

bolting.advice: Harvest all leaves now. Spinach bolts on long days regardless of heat.

kale.name: Kale

fallSow.notes: Fall planting for winter harvest - frost sweetens flavor

tips: Light frost improves kale flavor — sugars rise as the plant defends against cold. But a hard freeze below ~25°F can still damage the leaves, so pick before a hard freeze hits.
Harvest lower leaves first, plant keeps producing
Can overwinter in many zones
Multiple varieties: curly, lacinato, red Russian

bolting.advice: Kale is biennial — it normally only bolts in its SECOND year after going through winter (vernalization). First-year bolting is unusual and only happens under severe sustained heat (90°F+) or significant transplant shock. First-year flavor improves dramatically after light frost. Harvest outer leaves continuously.

swiss-chard.name: Swiss Chard
tips: Colorful stems are edible and decorative
More heat tolerant than spinach
Harvest outer leaves for continuous production
Can produce for entire growing season

bolting.advice: Swiss chard is biennial — first-year plants almost never bolt; the flower stalk appears in year 2 after overwintering. It's also more heat-tolerant than lettuce/spinach. If year-1 chard is shooting up, it's almost certainly transplant shock or extreme heat (90°F+). First-year leaves are tender; harvest outer stalks and the center keeps producing.

arugula.name: Arugula
tips: Fast growing - from seed to salad in 4 weeks
Peppery flavor intensifies in heat
Let some plants flower for edible blooms
Self-seeds readily for future crops

bolting.advice: Pull whole plants and use the flowers — they are peppery and great in salads.

collard-greens.name: Collard Greens
tips: Southern staple - extremely cold hardy
Frost sweetens the leaves
Harvest lower leaves, plant keeps producing
Can survive temperatures into the teens

bolting.advice: Collards are biennial — they normally bolt in year 2 after winter. They tolerate heat much better than lettuce or kale, but extreme sustained heat (90°F+) plus stress can trigger first-year bolting. Harvest outer leaves continuously; the plant keeps producing from the center.

mustard-greens.name: Mustard Greens
tips: Spicy, peppery flavor - milder when young
Very fast growing
Bolts quickly in heat - plant in spring or fall
Seeds can be saved for making mustard

bolting.advice: Mustard bolts fast — harvest young leaves, succession plant for continuous greens.

microgreens.name: Microgreens
tips: Grow year-round indoors on windowsill
Harvest when first true leaves appear
Concentrated nutrition - 4-40x more nutrients than mature plants
Popular varieties: sunflower, pea shoots, radish, broccoli

carrot.name: Carrots

fallSow.notes: Fall carrots sweeten after light frost

tips: Loose, sandy soil prevents forking
Keep soil moist during germination (up to 3 weeks)
Thin to 2-3 inches apart when tops are 2 inches tall
Fall carrots are sweetest after light frost

beet.name: Beets

fallSow.notes: Fall planting for late fall harvest

tips: Each seed cluster produces multiple plants - thin to one
Greens are edible and delicious
Harvest when golf ball to tennis ball size
Golden varieties don't bleed like red ones

bolting.advice: Beets bolt under sustained heat — harvest at tennis-ball size, pull the rest before flowering.

radish.name: Radishes
tips: Fastest vegetable to harvest - 3-4 weeks
Succession plant every week for continuous harvest
Becomes woody and pungent if left too long
Great for marking rows of slow-germinating seeds

bolting.advice: Pull any remaining radishes — the roots turn woody once the plant bolts.

potato.name: Potatoes
tips: Plant certified seed potatoes, not grocery store potatoes
Hill soil around stems as plants grow

TWO harvest windows: (1) NEW POTATOES — small, tender, thin-skinned. Ready when plants FLOWER (~60-70 days). Dig gently from the side of the hill; take a few and leave the rest growing. Eat within a week; do NOT cure or store. (2) STORAGE POTATOES — full-sized, thick-skinned. Wait until the plant TOPS DIE BACK naturally (~90-120 days). THEN cure in a dark, dry spot at 50-60°F for 2 weeks before storing. Pre-cured potatoes spoil fast.
Don't expose harvested potatoes to light — they turn green and develop solanine, which is bitter and mildly toxic

sweet-potato.name: Sweet Potatoes
tips: Grown from slips (rooted sprouts), not seed potatoes
Needs long warm season - 90+ frost-free days
Harvest before first frost
Cure at 80-85°F for 10 days for best storage

turnip.name: Turnips
tips: Both roots and greens are edible
Best flavor from fall harvest
Quick growing - good for succession planting
Harvest when 2-3 inches diameter

bolting.advice: Turnips get woody and bolt in heat. Harvest at golf-ball size for tender roots.

parsnip.name: Parsnips
tips: Needs frost to convert starches to sugar
Can overwinter in ground with mulch
Very slow germination - 3-4 weeks
Use fresh seed only - viability drops quickly

onion-yellow.name: Yellow Onion

fallSow.notes: Short-day onions (Vidalia, Texas Sweet) for zones 7+

tips: Day length determines bulbing - choose correct variety for latitude
Short-day onions (Vidalia) planted fall in zones 7+ for spring harvest
Stop watering when tops fall over
Cure in sun for 1-2 weeks before storage

onion-red.name: Red Onion
tips: Milder flavor than yellow onions
Great for fresh eating in salads
Shorter storage life than yellow varieties
Color fades when cooked

green-onion.name: Green Onions / Scallions
tips: Can regrow from root ends in water or soil
Harvest by pulling or cutting at soil level
Bunching varieties don't form bulbs
Succession plant for year-round harvest

garlic.name: Garlic
tips: Plant in fall for summer harvest
Separate bulbs into individual cloves for planting
Cut scapes (curly tops) to encourage larger bulbs
Harvest when lower leaves brown, tops still green

leek.name: Leeks
tips: Hill soil around stems for longer white portion
Very cold hardy - harvest into winter
Mild onion flavor, no bulb formation
Excellent in soups and stews

shallot.name: Shallots
tips: Plant sets (small bulbs) like garlic cloves
Each set multiplies into cluster of 4-12 shallots
Gourmet mild flavor prized by chefs
Store like onions after curing

broccoli.name: Broccoli

fallSow.notes: Start indoors mid-summer for fall transplant

tips: Harvest main head before flowers open
Side shoots continue producing for weeks after main harvest
Prefers cool weather - bolts in heat
Fall crop often easier than spring

bolting.advice: Harvest the central head now, before the buds open into yellow flowers. Side shoots will keep coming.

cauliflower.name: Cauliflower
tips: More finicky than broccoli - needs consistent conditions
Blanch white varieties by tying leaves over head
Purple and orange varieties don't need blanching
Single harvest per plant - no side shoots

bolting.advice: Cauliflower bolts fast in heat. Blanch heads (tie outer leaves over) once curd appears.

cabbage-green.name: Green Cabbage
tips: Very cold hardy - improves flavor after frost
Consistent watering prevents head splitting
Early varieties 70 days, storage varieties 100+ days
Excellent for sauerkraut

bolting.advice: Cabbage is biennial — it normally bolts in year 2 after winter. But the head can SPLIT in year 1 if heat hits before harvest, or if irrigation is uneven once heads are firm. That's not bolting, but the harvest signal is the same: cut at firmness, don't wait. If it does send up a year-1 flower stalk, that's heat-shock — harvest immediately, the head will be loose but edible.

cabbage-red.name: Red Cabbage
tips: Same care as green cabbage
Color intensifies in acidic conditions
Keeps color when pickled or fermented
High in antioxidants

bolting.advice: Red cabbage is biennial — it normally bolts in year 2 after winter. Year-1 heads can SPLIT under heat or uneven watering once firm. Harvest at firmness, don't wait. A year-1 flower stalk = heat shock; harvest immediately.

brussels-sprouts.name: Brussels Sprouts
tips: Frost dramatically improves flavor
Harvest bottom sprouts first as they mature
Top the plant 3 weeks before harvest for uniform sprouts
Long season - plant for fall/early winter harvest

bolting.advice: Brussels sprouts need cool weather to form sprouts. Heat triggers premature flowering.

kohlrabi.name: Kohlrabi
tips: Harvest when tennis ball size - larger gets woody
Swollen stem (not root) is the edible part
Mild, slightly sweet flavor like broccoli stem
Fast growing - good for succession planting

bolting.advice: Kohlrabi bulbs get woody and bitter once heat triggers bolting. Harvest at tennis-ball size.

bok-choy.name: Bok Choy
tips: Baby bok choy ready in 30 days
Bolts quickly in heat - grow in spring or fall
Both leaves and stems are edible
Great for stir-fries and salads

bolting.advice: Harvest whole plants now — quality drops fast after bolting starts.

green-beans.name: Green Beans
tips: Bush beans don't need support; pole beans need trellis
Pick frequently to encourage more production
Inoculant helps fix nitrogen in soil
Don't work in beds when leaves are wet - spreads disease

snap-peas.name: Sugar Snap Peas
tips: Plant as early as soil can be worked
Provide trellis - most varieties climb 4-6 feet
Entire pod is edible and sweet
Heat ends production - enjoy spring harvest

snow-peas.name: Snow Peas
tips: Harvest when pods are flat, before peas develop
Dwarf varieties available for small spaces
Tendrils and shoots also edible
Classic in stir-fries

shell-peas.name: Garden Peas
tips: Shell fresh peas immediately for best sweetness
Sugars convert to starch quickly after picking
Blanch and freeze for year-round enjoyment
Kids love eating them straight from the pod

lima-beans.name: Lima Beans
tips: Need warm soil - don't rush planting
Pole varieties more productive than bush
Harvest when pods are plump but still green
Also called butter beans

edamame.name: Edamame
tips: Harvest when pods are plump, still bright green
Entire plant produces at once - not continuous
Boil in salted water for classic preparation
High in protein - great vegetarian option

basil.name: Basil
tips: Pinch flower buds to prolong leaf production
Harvest from top to encourage bushy growth
Classic partner for tomatoes in garden and kitchen
Many varieties: sweet, Thai, lemon, purple
Basil roots easily in water — take a 4-inch cutting, strip lower leaves, put in a glass on the windowsill. Roots appear in 7-10 days; transplant when roots are 1 inch long. Free plants forever.

Leggy plant fix: if the basil is tall and sparse but still green, cut everything back by half (just above any leaf node). New shoots will emerge from every node below. If the lower stem is bare and brown, the plant won't recover — take tip cuttings instead and start fresh.
Storing harvested basil: stems in water like cut flowers, on the counter, NOT the fridge. Basil blackens below ~50°F. Use within 5 days fresh, or chop and freeze in olive oil for winter pesto base.

bolting.advice: Basil bolts mainly from heat stress (sustained 90°F+) and age, not just calendar days. Pinch flower spikes ½ inch below the lowest bud as they form to extend leaf production. Once half the plant is flowering, switch to harvesting the whole top — don't keep chasing individual flower stalks. In hot climates (zone 8+) it often bolts before day 75; in cooler zones it can hold till day 100.

pruning.title: Time to pinch your basil
pruning.message: Your basil should be at about 4–6 leaf pairs by now — if so, pinching the top now doubles the next round of harvest. (If it's still small or in transplant shock, give it another week.)
pruning.actionHint: Pinch ¼ inch above the topmost healthy pair of leaves — that pair stays. Two new shoots emerge from those leaf-armpits within 7–14 days. (If nothing happens in 2 weeks, the plant may be stressed — water consistently and try again on a fresh tip.)

cilantro.name: Cilantro
tips: Bolts quickly in heat - succession plant every 2 weeks
Seeds (coriander) are also culinary herb
Slow-bolt varieties extend harvest
Self-seeds readily for next year

bolting.advice: Harvest now. Cilantro flowers are edible (they become coriander seed) — let some go to seed for a free future crop.

parsley.name: Parsley
tips: Slow to germinate - soak seeds overnight
Biennial - flowers second year
Cut outer stems to encourage new growth
Flat-leaf has stronger flavor than curly

pruning.title: Outer-leaf harvest time
pruning.message: Always pick from the outside. Removing center leaves stops new growth — outer harvests keep the plant productive.
pruning.actionHint: Pick outer stems at the base. Leave the center alone.

mint.name: Mint
tips: VERY invasive - plant in containers or with barriers
Spreading roots can take over entire garden beds
Many varieties: spearmint, peppermint, chocolate, apple
Cut back hard to rejuvenate

pruning.title: Cut back your mint
pruning.message: A hard cutback keeps mint tender and prevents it from going woody and flowering.
pruning.actionHint: Cut the whole plant back by half. It will bounce back in about 10 days.

rosemary.name: Rosemary
tips: Mediterranean native - needs excellent drainage
Overwatering is the main killer
Bring indoors where winters are cold
Can live for decades in mild climates

pruning.title: Shape your rosemary
pruning.message: A light shaping keeps rosemary productive and prevents leggy growth.
pruning.actionHint: Trim up to a third of new green growth. Never cut into bare brown wood.

thyme.name: Thyme
tips: Very hardy once established
Prefers lean, dry conditions
Harvest before flowering for best flavor
Many varieties: lemon, caraway, creeping

pruning.title: Light trim for your thyme
pruning.message: Thyme gets woody without regular trimming.
pruning.actionHint: Snip the top third of each stem. Avoid cutting into hard wood.

oregano.name: Oregano
tips: Greek oregano has strongest flavor
Cut back after flowering to maintain shape
Dries very well for winter use
Spreads readily - can become aggressive

pruning.title: Cut back your oregano
pruning.message: Regular cutbacks keep oregano leafy instead of going to flower.
pruning.actionHint: Cut back by a third. Dry the trimmings for winter.

sage.name: Sage
tips: Beautiful silvery foliage - ornamental as well as culinary
Don't overwater - prefers dry conditions
Prune woody growth in spring to rejuvenate
Classic with poultry and stuffing

pruning.title: Pinch tips to keep bushy
pruning.message: Sage will get woody if not pinched. Regular tip-pinching encourages tender new growth.
pruning.actionHint: Pinch the top inch of growing tips. Use the trimmings.

dill.name: Dill
tips: Doesn't transplant well - direct sow only
Both leaves (dill weed) and seeds are flavorful
Self-seeds prolifically
Essential for pickles and fish dishes

bolting.advice: Let some plants flower for dill seed (and pollinators). Succession-sow for continuous fresh fronds.

chives.name: Chives
tips: Perennial that returns stronger each year
Purple flowers are edible and beautiful
Cut to 2 inches above ground to harvest
Divide clumps every 3-4 years

lavender.name: Lavender
tips: Needs excellent drainage - raised beds ideal
Don't overwater or over-fertilize
Harvest flowers just before fully open
Prune in spring to maintain shape

pruning.title: Annual lavender cutback
pruning.message: Lavender should be cut back ONCE per year — after the main bloom finishes in late summer. Cutting more often weakens the plant. CRITICAL: never cut into the bare brown wood at the base — lavender rarely regenerates from old wood and you can kill the plant.
pruning.actionHint: Wait until the year's bloom is done. Trim spent flower spikes and take up to ⅓ off the green foliage above. Stay in the green growth — if you can see bare brown stems, you've cut too deep. Stop and let it recover.

lemongrass.name: Lemongrass
tips: Tropical plant - bring indoors before frost
Can grow 3-6 feet tall
Harvest outer stalks at base
Root stalks from grocery store in water

strawberry.name: Strawberries
tips: June-bearing: one large harvest; everbearing: crops all season
Remove flowers first year for stronger plants
Runners can be rooted for new plants
Mulch heavily for winter protection

blueberry.name: Blueberries
tips: MUST have acidic soil - most gardens need amendment
Plant 2+ varieties for cross-pollination
Takes 2-3 years to produce significant harvest
Net plants to protect from birds

raspberry.name: Raspberries
tips: Summer-bearing vs everbearing varieties
Prune after fruiting to maintain productivity
Spreads via underground runners
Very productive once established

pruning.title: Annual cane pruning
pruning.message: Raspberry canes are biennial — first-year canes (primocanes) bear next year's crop. Remove second-year canes (floricanes) after harvest.
pruning.actionHint: After harvest, cut floricanes to ground. Thin primocanes to ~6 per linear foot.

blackberry.name: Blackberries
tips: Thornless varieties available for easier harvest
Train on trellis or fence
Can become invasive if not managed
Prune after harvest - new canes fruit next year

pruning.title: Annual cane pruning
pruning.message: Blackberry canes are biennial — second-year canes bear, then die. Remove spent canes after harvest, train primocanes for next year.
pruning.actionHint: Cut spent floricanes to ground after harvest. Tie up primocanes.

grape.name: Grapes
tips: Needs sturdy trellis or arbor support
Heavy pruning essential for fruit production
Takes 3 years to first real harvest
American, European, and hybrid varieties for different climates

apple.name: Apples
tips: Plant 2+ varieties for pollination
Dwarf rootstocks for smaller trees
Annual pruning essential
Takes 4-7 years to first fruit

pear.name: Pears
tips: Harvest when still firm, ripen off tree
Less maintenance than apples
Fire blight can be serious issue
Asian pears ripen on tree, European off tree

peach.name: Peaches
tips: Need 600-900 chill hours depending on variety
Self-fertile - one tree sufficient
Thin fruit to 6-8 inches apart for larger peaches
Prune heavily - fruits on 1-year wood

plum.name: Plums
tips: European plums often self-fertile; Japanese need pollinators
Less prone to pests than peaches
Prunes are dried Italian plums
Thin fruit for larger plums

cherry.name: Cherries
tips: Sweet cherries need pollinators; sour often self-fertile
Bird netting essential
Short harvest window - pick when fully colored
Rain during harvest causes splitting

fig.name: Figs
tips: Can grow in containers and overwinter indoors
Wrap or protect in cold climates
Self-pollinating - one tree is enough
Two crops possible in warm climates

lemon.name: Lemons
tips: Frost-sensitive - zones 9-11 outdoors only
Meyer lemons best for containers
Bring indoors where winters are cold
Fruit year-round once established

lime.name: Limes
tips: More cold-sensitive than lemons
Key limes best for containers
Thornless varieties available
Harvest when green for most lime flavor

orange.name: Oranges
tips: Need consistent warmth - zones 9-11
Navel for eating, Valencia for juice
Fruit may stay on tree for months
Dwarf varieties good for containers

corn.name: Sweet Corn
tips: Plant in blocks, not rows, for pollination
Heavy feeder - needs nitrogen
Silks dry and brown when ready — this is the EARLIEST signal, not the only one

MILK STAGE TEST (most reliable): peel back a small section of husk on an ear that looks ready. Pierce a kernel with your thumbnail. CLEAR liquid = too early. MILKY liquid = perfect, pick now. DOUGHY/STARCHY (no liquid) = past prime, kernels will be tough. Check every 1-2 days once silks brown.
Sugars convert to starch quickly - eat or freeze same day

asparagus.name: Asparagus
tips: Perennial that produces for 20+ years
Don't harvest first 2 years
Harvest in spring for 6-8 weeks
Let ferns grow to feed roots for next year

rhubarb.name: Rhubarb
tips: Only stalks edible - leaves are toxic
Don't harvest first year
Pull (don't cut) stalks for harvest
Needs winter cold to produce well

artichoke.name: Artichokes
tips: Perennial in mild climates, annual elsewhere
Needs vernalization (cold period) to flower
Harvest buds before they open
Large, dramatic plants - good focal point

celery.name: Celery
tips: Very demanding - needs constant moisture
Blanch by mounding soil or wrapping
Slow growing but worth the effort
Harvest outer stalks as needed

fennel.name: Fennel
tips: Don't plant near other vegetables - can inhibit growth
Bulb fennel vs herb fennel are different
Bolts in heat - plant for spring or fall
Anise flavor mellows when cooked

bolting.advice: Bulb fennel bolts under heat or long days. Harvest bulbs as soon as they swell to fist size.

okra.name: Okra
tips: Loves heat - don't rush planting
Soak seeds overnight for better germination
Harvest at 3-4 inches - larger pods get tough
Pick every 2-3 days for continuous production

sprouts.name: Sprouts
tips: No soil needed - just water and a jar
Rinse 2-3 times daily
Popular: mung bean, alfalfa, broccoli, radish
Refrigerate and eat within a week

flowers-edible.name: Edible Flowers
tips: Popular: nasturtiums, pansies, calendula, borage
Never eat flowers treated with pesticides
Harvest in morning after dew dries
Add color and flavor to salads and desserts

flowers-cut.name: Cut Flowers
tips: Popular: zinnias, sunflowers, dahlias, cosmos
Cut in early morning for longest vase life
Cut more for more blooms
Succession plant for season-long bouquets

notes.timingExplanation: All timing is relative to last spring frost date unless otherwise noted. Negative weeks = before frost, positive = after.

frostTolerance.high: Very cold hardy, can survive temps below 20°F

notes.perennials: Berries and tree fruits focus on care/harvest timing rather than planting from seed. Days to maturity represents time to first significant harvest.
notes.tropicalNote: For tropical regions, crops marked tropicalSuitable:false may not grow well due to lack of cold period or excessive heat.
